draft.introduction: In Metaphysics XII.9, Aristotle asks what the divine intellect thinks. Since it must think the best object, and it is itself the best object, God’s activity is “thinking of thinking” (noesis noeseos)—self-contemplation without end.

translation.text: The divine thought thinks itself, if it is the most excellent of things, and its thinking is a thinking of thinking. Mind thinks itself through participation in the object of thought; for it becomes an object of thought in coming into contact with and thinking its objects, so that mind and the object of thought are the same.
translation.original_text: αὑτὸν ἄρα νοεῖ, εἴπερ ἐστὶ τὸ κράτιστον, καὶ ἔστιν ἡ νόησις νοήσεως νόησις

translator_notes.note: The formula noesis noeseos noesis (νόησις νοήσεως νόησις) became the definitive description of divine activity in the Aristotelian and Neoplatonic traditions. Plotinus relocated this self-thinking to the level of Nous, subordinate to the One which transcends even thought.
